evidence: projects, located in Ohio and Utah, represent a combined 1.9 gigawatts of total capacity and are backed by up to 12.5 year, primarily fixed-price agreements

Commitments for construction and acquisition of property, plant, and equipment for Williams...are approximately $4.3 billion...A significant portion...relates to acquisition of long-lead time equipment for the data center power innovation projects
Williams is investing in construction projects to support the power demands created by new data center and industrial development in power grid-constrained markets
Williams plans to place the projects into service during 2026 through 2028, assuming timely receipt of permits, and continues to pursue additional projects
accurately predicting future power needs of data centers due to rapidly changing technology and market dynamics, which could result in underutilized or stranded assets
risks that may be enhanced through the use of artificial intelligence, which could include threats to operational industrial control systems and safety systems that operate its pipelines

financing the capital investment needed to build and maintain the necessary infrastructure to support data center development; managing community opposition; managing the possible environmental impact

capex_mentions.quote: Commitments for construction and acquisition of property, plant, and equipment for Williams...are approximately $4.3 billion
capex_mentions.context: Total construction commitments at December 31, 2025; significant portion for data center power innovation projects

limitations: Williams is an energy infrastructure company investing in power generation for data centers — AI investment here is indirect (supplying power to AI/data center customers), not direct AI capex; scores reflect this indirect exposure
The $4.3 billion commitment figure covers all construction commitments, not exclusively data center/AI-related projects; exact AI-attributable share is not isolated
AI narrative concreteness is limited to infrastructure deployment for data center customers; no internal AI use cases or AI-driven operational tools are described in the excerpts
CIO background mentioning 'enterprise artificial intelligence initiatives' is biographical context, not a disclosed AI program
